examples.explanation: Input: n = 5 (binary 101). Step 1: n = 5, count = 0. Check LSB: 5 & 1 = 1 (since 101 & 001 = 001), so count = 1. Right-shift: n = 5 >> 1 = 2 (binary 10). Step 2: n = 2, count = 1. Check LSB: 2 & 1 = 0 (10 & 01 = 00), so count remains 1. Right-shift: n = 2 >> 1 = 1 (binary 1). Step 3: n = 1, count = 1. Check LSB: 1 & 1 = 1 (1 & 1 = 1), so count = 2. Right-shift: n = 1 >> 1 = 0. Step 4: n = 0, loop ends. Return count = 2.

Input: n = 7 (binary 111). Step 1: n = 7, count = 0. Check LSB: 7 & 1 = 1 (111 & 001 = 001), count = 1. Right-shift: n = 7 >> 1 = 3 (binary 11). Step 2: n = 3, count = 1. Check LSB: 3 & 1 = 1 (11 & 01 = 01), count = 2. Right-shift: n = 3 >> 1 = 1 (binary 1). Step 3: n = 1, count = 2. Check LSB: 1 & 1 = 1, count = 3. Right-shift: n = 1 >> 1 = 0. Step 4: n = 0, loop ends. Return count = 3.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n) where n is bits
approaches.summary: Iterate through each bit of the integer by checking the least significant bit and right-shifting until the number becomes zero. This is straightforward and easy to implement for any integer size.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(log n) where n is the integer value, or O(b) where b is the number of bits (e.g., 32 for 32-bit integers)

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count Set Bits in Integer Using Brute Force
ladders.desc: Complete implementation counting 1s in binary representation by iterating through bits.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires counting the number of '1' bits (set bits) in the binary representation of a given integer. The brute force approach is intuitive: we examine each bit individually. The key insight is that we can repeatedly check the least significant bit (LSB) of the number and then eliminate it by right-shifting the number. Specifically, we use a while loop that continues as long as the number is non-zero. In each iteration, we check if the LSB is 1 by using the bitwise AND operation with 1 (n & 1). If it is 1, we increment our count. Then, we right-shift the number by 1 bit (n >>= 1) to discard the LSB and bring the next bit into the LSB position. This process repeats until all bits have been examined (when n becomes 0). The time complexity is O(b), where b is the number of bits in the integer (e.g., 32 for a 32-bit integer), which is equivalent to O(log n) since b ≈ log₂(n) for positive n. Space complexity is O(1) as we only use a few variables. This approach is straightforward and works for both positive and negative integers if we consider two's complement representation, though for simplicity, we often assume non-negative inputs. Common mistakes include infinite loops if not handling negative numbers properly (though right-shift in most languages handles signed integers correctly), or off-by-one errors in the loop condition. The connection to higher levels is direct: this is the complete solution using the brute force method, building on the sub-routines and logic from lower levels. Real code pattern in Python-style pseudocode: def count_set_bits(n): count = 0; while n: count += n & 1; n >>= 1; return count.

examples.trace: Input: n = 5 (binary 101). Step 1: n = 5, count = 0. Check LSB: 5 & 1 = 1 (since 101 & 001 = 001), so count = 1. Right-shift: n = 5 >> 1 = 2 (binary 10). Step 2: n = 2, count = 1. Check LSB: 2 & 1 = 0 (10 & 01 = 00), so count remains 1. Right-shift: n = 2 >> 1 = 1 (binary 1). Step 3: n = 1, count = 1. Check LSB: 1 & 1 = 1 (1 & 1 = 1), so count = 2. Right-shift: n = 1 >> 1 = 0. Step 4: n = 0, loop ends. Return count = 2.

Input: n = 7 (binary 111). Step 1: n = 7, count = 0. Check LSB: 7 & 1 = 1 (111 & 001 = 001), count = 1. Right-shift: n = 7 >> 1 = 3 (binary 11). Step 2: n = 3, count = 1. Check LSB: 3 & 1 = 1 (11 & 01 = 01), count = 2. Right-shift: n = 3 >> 1 = 1 (binary 1). Step 3: n = 1, count = 2. Check LSB: 1 & 1 = 1, count = 3. Right-shift: n = 1 >> 1 = 0. Step 4: n = 0, loop ends. Return count = 3.

ladders.title: Key Sub-Routine — Iterate and Count Bits with While Loop
ladders.desc: Implement the loop that processes each bit until the number becomes zero.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the brute force approach: a while loop that iterates through each bit of the integer. The intuition is to repeatedly examine and remove the least significant bit (LSB) until no bits remain. We start with the input integer n and a counter initialized to 0. The loop condition is while n != 0, ensuring we stop when all bits have been processed. Inside the loop, we perform two key operations: first, check if the LSB is 1 using n & 1, and if so, increment the counter; second, right-shift n by 1 bit using n = n >> 1 to discard the LSB and bring the next bit into position. This process effectively traverses the binary representation from right to left (LSB to MSB). The time complexity is O(b), where b is the number of bits, as each iteration processes one bit. Space complexity is O(1) since we only use a constant amount of extra memory. This sub-routine connects to the full problem (L0) as it encapsulates the main algorithmic flow; the full problem simply wraps this in a function with input/output handling. Common mistakes include using a for loop with a fixed number of iterations (which may be inefficient or incorrect for varying bit lengths), or not updating n correctly in the loop. The intuition behind the loop is that each right-shift reduces n by half in terms of bit length, making it a logarithmic process in the value of n. Real code pattern: while n: # process LSB and shift.

examples.trace: Input: n = 6 (binary 110). Initialize count = 0. Iteration 1: n = 6, n & 1 = 0 (110 & 001 = 000), count remains 0. Right-shift: n = 6 >> 1 = 3 (binary 11). Iteration 2: n = 3, n & 1 = 1 (11 & 01 = 01), count = 1. Right-shift: n = 3 >> 1 = 1 (binary 1). Iteration 3: n = 1, n & 1 = 1, count = 2. Right-shift: n = 1 >> 1 = 0. Loop ends as n = 0. Total iterations: 3, final count = 2.

Input: n = 8 (binary 1000). Initialize count = 0. Iteration 1: n = 8, n & 1 = 0 (1000 & 0001 = 0000), count = 0. Right-shift: n = 8 >> 1 = 4 (binary 100). Iteration 2: n = 4, n & 1 = 0, count = 0. Right-shift: n = 4 >> 1 = 2 (binary 10). Iteration 3: n = 2, n & 1 = 0, count = 0. Right-shift: n = 2 >> 1 = 1 (binary 1). Iteration 4: n = 1, n & 1 = 1, count = 1. Right-shift: n = 1 >> 1 = 0. Loop ends. Total iterations: 4, final count = 1.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Check LSB and Right-Shift to Process Bits
ladders.desc: The central technique of examining the least significant bit and shifting to next bit.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the core logic of the brute force approach: how to isolate and check the least significant bit (LSB), and then move to the next bit. The intuition is that in binary representation, the LSB (rightmost bit) determines whether the number is odd or even (1 for odd, 0 for even). We can extract this bit using the bitwise AND operation with 1 (n & 1). This works because 1 in binary is ...0001, so AND-ing with n zeroes out all bits except the LSB, leaving either 0 or 1. After checking, we need to 'remove' this bit to process the next one. This is done by right-shifting the number by 1 bit (n >> 1), which discards the LSB and shifts all other bits to the right, effectively dividing the number by 2 in integer arithmetic. For example, if n is 1011 (11 in decimal), n & 1 gives 1 (since LSB is 1), and n >> 1 gives 101 (5 in decimal). Repeating this process allows us to traverse all bits from LSB to MSB. The time complexity per operation is O(1), and we do this for each bit, leading to O(b) overall. Space is O(1). This logic connects to L1 as it forms the body of the while loop; L1 uses this repeatedly. Common mistakes include confusing bitwise AND with logical AND, or not understanding that right-shift on negative numbers may have language-specific behavior (in Python, it preserves sign). The intuition is that we're peeling off bits one by one, like checking digits in a number. Real code pattern: bit = n & 1; n = n >> 1.

examples.trace: Input: n = 9 (binary 1001). Step 1: Check LSB: n & 1 = 9 & 1 = 1 (since 1001 & 0001 = 0001). Right-shift: n = 9 >> 1 = 4 (binary 100). Step 2: Check LSB: n & 1 = 4 & 1 = 0 (100 & 001 = 000). Right-shift: n = 4 >> 1 = 2 (binary 10). Step 3: Check LSB: n & 1 = 2 & 1 = 0. Right-shift: n = 2 >> 1 = 1 (binary 1). Step 4: Check LSB: n & 1 = 1 & 1 = 1. Right-shift: n = 1 >> 1 = 0. Process ends. LSB checks: [1,0,0,1], n after each shift: [4,2,1,0].

ladders.title: Building Block — Bitwise AND Operation to Isolate Bits
ladders.desc: Using bitwise AND to extract specific bits from a number.
ladders.explanation: This building block introduces the bitwise AND operation, which is crucial for checking the least significant bit (LSB). Bitwise AND operates on the binary representations of two integers, comparing each corresponding bit: the result bit is 1 only if both input bits are 1, otherwise 0. For example, to isolate the LSB of a number n, we compute n & 1. Since 1 in binary is ...0001, this operation zeroes out all bits except the LSB. If n's LSB is 1, n & 1 yields 1; if it's 0, it yields 0. This works because AND with 1 acts as a mask that 'lets through' only the LSB. The intuition is that we're filtering the number to see if it's odd (LSB = 1) or even (LSB = 0). In the context of counting set bits, we use this to check each bit individually as we shift through the number. Time complexity is O(1) per operation, and space is O(1). This connects to L2 as it's the first part of the core logic (checking LSB). Common mistakes include using logical AND (&&) instead of bitwise AND (&), or not understanding that n & 1 returns an integer (0 or 1), not a boolean. Another pitfall is assuming it works only for positive numbers; it works for negatives in two's complement as well, but care is needed with sign bits. The key insight is that bitwise operations allow direct manipulation of binary data. Real code pattern: result = n & 1.

examples.trace: Input: n = 5 (binary 101). Compute n & 1: 101 AND 001 = 001. In decimal, 5 & 1 = 1. This shows the LSB is 1, meaning 5 is odd.
Input: n = 12 (binary 1100). Compute n & 1: 1100 AND 0001 = 0000. In decimal, 12 & 1 = 0. This shows the LSB is 0, meaning 12 is even.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Right-Shift to Divide by Two
ladders.desc: Using right-shift operator to move bits and effectively divide integer by 2.
ladders.explanation: This level covers the right-shift operation (>>), which is essential for moving to the next bit in the brute force approach. Right-shifting an integer by k positions moves all bits to the right, discarding the k least significant bits and filling leftmost bits with zeros (for unsigned numbers) or sign bits (for signed numbers in some languages). Specifically, n >> 1 shifts n by 1 bit to the right. In binary, this is equivalent to removing the LSB and shifting all other bits rightward. For example, if n is 1011 (11 decimal), n >> 1 gives 101 (5 decimal). Mathematically, this is integer division by 2, discarding any remainder (since the LSB, which represents the remainder, is dropped). The intuition is that we're 'consuming' bits from the right as we process them. In the context of counting set bits, after checking the LSB, we right-shift to bring the next bit into the LSB position for the next iteration. Time complexity is O(1) per shift, and space is O(1). This connects to L2 as it's the second part of the core logic (shifting to next bit). Common mistakes include using right-shift on negative numbers without understanding language-specific behavior (e.g., in Python, it preserves sign, so -1 >> 1 = -1), or confusing it with division (though equivalent for non-negatives). The key insight is that right-shift is a fast bit-level operation. Real code pattern: n = n >> 1.

examples.trace: Input: n = 6 (binary 110). Right-shift by 1: move all bits right, discard LSB (0), result is 11 (binary), which is 3 in decimal. So, 6 >> 1 = 3. This is equivalent to integer division: 6 // 2 = 3.
Input: n = 13 (binary 1101). Right-shift by 1: discard LSB (1), shift others right to get 110 (binary), which is 6 in decimal. So, 13 >> 1 = 6. Integer division: 13 // 2 = 6.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Binary Representation of Integers
ladders.desc: Understanding how integers are represented in binary (base-2) system.
ladders.explanation: This foundational level introduces binary representation, which is prerequisite for counting set bits. In the binary system, numbers are expressed using only two digits: 0 and 1. Each digit is called a bit, and the position of a bit represents a power of 2. For example, the binary number 1011 represents 1*2^3 + 0*2^2 + 1*2^1 + 1*2^0 = 8 + 0 + 2 + 1 = 11 in decimal. The rightmost bit is the least significant bit (LSB), corresponding to 2^0, and bits to the left increase in significance. A set bit is a bit with value 1. The intuition for counting set bits is that we need to count how many 1s appear in this binary representation. This concept connects to L4 and above because operations like bitwise AND and right-shift directly manipulate these binary digits. For instance, checking the LSB with n & 1 relies on knowing that the LSB determines odd/even status. Time and space are not applicable here as it's a conceptual foundation. Common mistakes include confusing binary with decimal, or not understanding that leading zeros don't change the value (e.g., 00101 is same as 101). The key insight is that computers store integers in binary, so bitwise operations are natural for such problems. Real code pattern: understanding that bin(n) in Python gives binary string.

examples.trace: Input: decimal 5. Convert to binary: 5 divided by 2 gives quotient 2, remainder 1 (LSB). 2 divided by 2 gives quotient 1, remainder 0. 1 divided by 2 gives quotient 0, remainder 1. Reading remainders from last to first: 101. So, 5 in binary is 101, with set bits at positions 2^0 and 2^2.

Input: decimal 12. Convert to binary: 12 / 2 = 6 rem 0 (LSB). 6 / 2 = 3 rem 0. 3 / 2 = 1 rem 1. 1 / 2 = 0 rem 1. Remainders: 0,0,1,1, reverse to 1100. So, 12 in binary is 1100, with set bits at positions 2^2 and 2^3.

approaches.name: Brian Kernighan's Algorithm O(k) where k is set bits
approaches.summary: Repeatedly clear the least significant set bit using n & (n-1) until n becomes zero, counting each operation. This skips over zeros between set bits, improving efficiency when set bits are sparse.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(k) where k is the number of set bits

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count Set Bits Using Brian Kernighan's Algorithm
ladders.desc: Complete implementation counting 1s in binary representation using n & (n-1) pattern
ladders.explanation: Brian Kernighan's algorithm provides an optimized solution for counting set bits by leveraging the mathematical property that n & (n-1) clears the least significant set bit. The intuition comes from observing binary subtraction: when you subtract 1 from a number, all bits after the least significant set bit flip from 0 to 1, and the least significant set bit becomes 0. The bitwise AND with the original number then clears exactly that least significant set bit while preserving all other bits. This allows us to count set bits by repeatedly clearing them until the number becomes zero, with each operation counting exactly one set bit. The algorithm works because each iteration removes exactly one set bit, and we stop when no bits remain. Time complexity is O(k) where k is the number of set bits, which is optimal when bits are sparse. Space complexity is O(1) as we only use a counter variable. This approach is superior to checking each bit individually (O(log n)) when numbers have few set bits. The connection to L1 is that this is the complete application of the key subroutine. Common mistakes include infinite loops from not updating n properly, off-by-one errors in counting, and misunderstanding the termination condition. The algorithm elegantly demonstrates how bit manipulation can optimize seemingly simple problems.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Clear Least Significant Set Bit
ladders.desc: Implement n & (n-1) operation that clears the rightmost 1 bit
ladders.explanation: The core operation n = n & (n-1) is the heart of Brian Kernighan's algorithm. The intuition is based on binary representation properties: when we subtract 1 from a number, all bits to the right of the least significant set bit flip from 0 to 1, and the least significant set bit itself becomes 0. When we perform a bitwise AND between the original number and this subtracted value, all bits that were 1 in both numbers remain 1, but the least significant set bit (which became 0 in n-1) becomes 0 in the result. All bits to the right of it were 0 in the original number, so they remain 0. Bits to the left remain unchanged. This operation therefore clears exactly one set bit - the rightmost one. The algorithm is: given integer n, compute n_minus_one = n - 1, then result = n & n_minus_one. Time complexity is O(1) as it's a constant-time bit operation. Space complexity is O(1). This feeds into L0 by being the repeated operation that counts bits. Common mistakes include forgetting that n must be positive for this to work correctly with subtraction, misunderstanding that this clears only ONE bit (not all bits), and not recognizing that n must be updated with the result. The operation works because of how binary numbers represent values and how subtraction affects bit patterns.

examples.trace:
n = 12 (binary 1100)
n-1 = 11 (binary 1011)
n & (n-1) = 1100 & 1011
Bitwise AND:
Position 3: 1 & 1 = 1
Position 2: 1 & 0 = 0
Position 1: 0 & 1 = 0
Position 0: 0 & 1 = 0
Result: 1000 = 8
The rightmost 1 bit (at position 2) was cleared.

n = 7 (binary 0111)
n-1 = 6 (binary 0110)
n & (n-1) = 0111 & 0110
Bitwise AND:
Position 2: 1 & 1 = 1
Position 1: 1 & 1 = 1
Position 0: 1 & 0 = 0
Result: 0110 = 6
The rightmost 1 bit (at position 0) was cleared.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Understanding n & (n-1) Pattern
ladders.desc: Analyze why n & (n-1) clears the least significant set bit
ladders.explanation: The fundamental insight is that subtracting 1 from a binary number flips all bits from the least significant set bit to the rightmost bit. Consider any binary number: it has a rightmost 1 bit at some position k, with zeros to its right (if any). When we subtract 1, we need to borrow from position k, making it 0, and all bits to the right become 1 (since we're effectively subtracting 1 from a string of zeros, which requires borrowing and creates ones). The bitwise AND then preserves bits where both numbers have 1s. Since position k became 0 in n-1, it becomes 0 in the result. Bits to the right of k were 0 in n and 1 in n-1, so they become 0. Bits to the left of k remain unchanged in both numbers. This mathematical property is what makes the algorithm work. The intuition is that subtraction affects bits in a predictable way that can be leveraged with bitwise operations. This feeds into L1 as the theoretical foundation for the subroutine. Time analysis is conceptual - understanding this pattern is constant-time insight. Space is not applicable. Common mistakes include not considering edge cases (n=0), misunderstanding which bit gets cleared (it's always the rightmost 1), and not recognizing that this works for any positive integer. The pattern reveals how binary arithmetic and bitwise operations interact elegantly.

examples.trace:
Original: 101100
Rightmost 1 is at position 2 (from right, 0-indexed)
Bits to right: positions 0-1 are 00
n-1: Subtract 1:
- Position 2: 1 becomes 0 (we borrow)
- Positions 0-1: 00 become 11 (after borrowing)
- Positions left of 2: unchanged
So n-1 = 101011
Now n & (n-1):
101100 & 101011 = 101000
Indeed, rightmost 1 (at position 2) cleared, result is 101000

Original: 1000 (binary for 8)
Rightmost 1 is at position 3
Bits to right: positions 0-2 are 000
n-1: Subtract 1:
- Position 3: 1 becomes 0
- Positions 0-2: 000 become 111
So n-1 = 0111
Now n & (n-1):
1000 & 0111 = 0000
Rightmost 1 cleared, all bits become 0

ladders.title: Building Block — Bitwise AND Operation
ladders.desc: Master the bitwise AND operator and its properties
ladders.explanation: The bitwise AND operator (&) compares corresponding bits of two numbers and returns 1 only if both bits are 1, otherwise returns 0. This is a fundamental building block because Brian Kernighan's algorithm relies on using AND to selectively clear bits. The intuition is that AND acts as a mask: when you AND a number with another number that has 0s in specific positions, those positions become 0 in the result, while positions where the mask has 1s preserve the original bit. In n & (n-1), the (n-1) serves as a dynamic mask that has 0 exactly at the position of the least significant set bit in n. The algorithm uses this property to clear that specific bit. Understanding AND requires knowing binary representation: each integer is represented as bits (0s and 1s), and AND operates on each bit position independently. Time complexity of AND is O(1) at machine level (though conceptually O(log n) bits). Space is O(1). This feeds into L2 as the operator that implements the clearing. Common mistakes include confusing & with logical AND (&&), not understanding that AND is performed bit-by-bit, and forgetting that results are in binary. Real pattern: result = a & b where each bit_i = a_bit_i AND b_bit_i. AND has properties: a & a = a (identity), a & 0 = 0 (clearing), a & 1 = a (preserving when 1).

examples.trace:
a = 13 = binary 1101
b = 11 = binary 1011
Bitwise AND:
Position 3: 1 & 1 = 1
Position 2: 1 & 0 = 0
Position 1: 0 & 1 = 0
Position 0: 1 & 1 = 1
Result: 1001 = 9
Each bit compared: only positions 3 and 0 had 1 in both numbers.

a = 6 = binary 0110
b = 5 = binary 0101
Bitwise AND:
Position 3: 0 & 0 = 0
Position 2: 1 & 1 = 1
Position 1: 1 & 0 = 0
Position 0: 0 & 1 = 0
Result: 0100 = 4
Only position 2 had 1 in both numbers.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Binary Subtraction by 1
ladders.desc: Understand how subtracting 1 affects binary representation
ladders.explanation: Subtracting 1 from a binary number has a predictable effect: it flips all bits from the least significant set bit (rightmost 1) to the least significant bit (rightmost position). The intuition is based on how borrowing works in binary subtraction. When you subtract 1 from a number, if the least significant bit is 1, it simply becomes 0. If it's 0, you need to borrow from the next higher bit that's 1. This borrowing causes that bit to become 0, and all bits between it and the original least significant bit become 1. More formally: for any binary number n, let k be the position of the least significant 1 bit (0-indexed from right). Then n-1 will have: bits at positions > k unchanged, bit at position k becomes 0, and bits at positions < k become 1. This pattern is crucial because when we later AND n with n-1, the bit at position k becomes 0 (since it's 1 in n and 0 in n-1), while bits at positions < k become 0 (since they're 0 in n and 1 in n-1). Time complexity is O(1) for subtraction operation. Space is O(1). This feeds into L3 as it creates one operand for the AND operation. Common mistakes include not considering n=0 case (subtraction underflow), misunderstanding which bits change, and not connecting this to the borrowing process. The operation is fundamental to binary arithmetic.

examples.trace:
n = 12 = binary 1100
Least significant 1 is at position 2 (third from right)
Subtract 1:
- Look at position 0: 0 - 1, need borrow
- Borrow from position 1: 0, need borrow further
- Borrow from position 2: 1 becomes 0
- Positions 0-1 become 1 (after borrowing)
- Position 3 unchanged
Result: 1011 = 11
Bits from position 2 to 0 flipped: 100 became 011

n = 7 = binary 0111
Least significant 1 is at position 0
Subtract 1:
- Position 0: 1 - 1 = 0
- Positions 1-2 unchanged
Result: 0110 = 6
Only position 0 changed from 1 to 0

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Binary Number Representation
ladders.desc: Understand how integers are represented in binary (base-2)
ladders.explanation: Binary representation is the foundation of all bit manipulation algorithms. In binary (base-2), numbers are represented using only two digits: 0 and 1. Each position represents a power of 2, starting from 2^0 at the rightmost position. For example, binary 1011 represents: 1*2^3 + 0*2^2 + 1*2^1 + 1*2^0 = 8 + 0 + 2 + 1 = 11. The intuition is that computers store integers in binary format, allowing us to manipulate individual bits. A 'set bit' refers to a bit that is 1. Counting set bits means counting how many 1s are in the binary representation. This is fundamental because Brian Kernighan's algorithm operates directly on this binary representation. Understanding binary requires knowing: bits are numbered from right (least significant) to left (most significant), each bit position doubles in value, and converting between decimal and binary. Time complexity for understanding is conceptual. Space is not applicable. This feeds into L4 as the representation system we operate on. Common mistakes include confusing bit positions (starting from 0 or 1), misunderstanding that binary is positional like decimal but with base 2, and not practicing conversions. Beginners should practice converting small numbers between decimal and binary to build intuition.

examples.trace:
5 in binary:
Find largest power of 2 ≤ 5: 4 = 2^2
5 - 4 = 1
Next power: 2 = 2^1, but 2 > 1, so use 0
Next power: 1 = 2^0, 1 - 1 = 0
So bits: position 2: 1, position 1: 0, position 0: 1
Binary: 101
Check: 1*4 + 0*2 + 1*1 = 5

10 in binary:
Largest power of 2 ≤ 10: 8 = 2^3
10 - 8 = 2
Next power: 4 = 2^2, but 4 > 2, so use 0
Next power: 2 = 2^1, 2 - 2 = 0
Next power: 1 = 2^0, 0 - 1 (can't), so use 0
So bits: position 3: 1, position 2: 0, position 1: 1, position 0: 0
Binary: 1010
Check: 1*8 + 0*4 + 1*2 + 0*1 = 10

approaches.name: Lookup Table O(1) per byte or word
approaches.summary: Precompute the number of set bits for all possible byte values (0-255) and then count by breaking the integer into bytes and summing the precomputed counts. This trades space for time, especially effective for large or repeated queries.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(1) per integer assuming fixed size (e.g., O(4) for 32-bit integers)

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count Set Bits Using Lookup Table
ladders.desc: Complete solution counting 1s in binary representation using precomputed byte table
ladders.explanation: This approach solves the problem of counting set bits (1s) in an integer's binary representation by trading space for time efficiency. The intuition is that we can precompute the number of set bits for all possible byte values (0-255) once, then for any integer, we break it into bytes and sum the precomputed counts. This is especially effective for large or repeated queries since the table lookup is O(1) per byte. For a 32-bit integer, we process 4 bytes, making it O(4) operations regardless of the integer's value. The algorithm: 1. Precompute a 256-element array where table[i] = number of 1s in binary of i. 2. For input integer n, initialize count = 0. 3. While n != 0: extract the lowest 8 bits (n & 0xFF), add table[extracted_byte] to count, right shift n by 8 bits (n >>= 8). 4. Return count. Time complexity is O(k) where k is number of bytes (e.g., 4 for 32-bit), effectively O(1) for fixed-size integers. Space is O(256) for the table. This connects to L1 by using the byte extraction and table lookup as the key subroutine. Common mistakes include forgetting to handle negative numbers properly (use unsigned operations or mask), not precomputing the table efficiently (can use DP: table[i] = table[i>>1] + (i&1)), and infinite loops if not checking while n != 0. Real code pattern: def count_set_bits(n): table = [0]*256; for i in range(256): table[i] = table[i>>1] + (i&1); count = 0; while n: count += table[n & 0xFF]; n >>= 8; return count.

examples.trace: Input: n = 42 (binary 00101010). Step 1: Precomputed table ready (e.g., table[42] = 3). Step 2: Initialize count = 0. Step 3: First iteration: n = 42, extract lowest byte: 42 & 0xFF = 42, count += table[42] = 0+3 = 3, n >>= 8 gives n = 0. Step 4: n = 0, loop ends. Return count = 3.

Input: n = 255 (binary 11111111). Step 1: Precomputed table ready (table[255] = 8). Step 2: count = 0. Step 3: First iteration: n = 255, extract byte: 255 & 0xFF = 255, count += table[255] = 0+8 = 8, n >>= 8 gives n = 0. Step 4: n = 0, loop ends. Return count = 8.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Extract Byte and Lookup in Precomputed Table
ladders.desc: Helper function to get lowest byte of integer and retrieve its set bit count from table
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core of the lookup table approach, performing the repeated operation in the main loop. The intuition is that by isolating the lowest 8 bits (one byte) of the integer, we can use it as an index into a precomputed table that gives the count of 1s for that specific byte value. This avoids recalculating the bit count each time. The algorithm: 1. Input: integer n and precomputed table array of size 256. 2. Extract the lowest byte: byte_val = n & 0xFF (0xFF is binary 11111111, masking keeps only last 8 bits). 3. Look up count: bit_count = table[byte_val]. 4. Return bit_count. Time complexity is O(1) for the extraction and lookup. Space is O(1) plus the table. This feeds into L0 by being called in a loop for each byte of the integer. Common mistakes include using wrong mask (e.g., 0xFF for 8 bits, ensure it matches byte size), not handling signed integers correctly (use unsigned operations to avoid sign extension), and assuming table is indexed correctly (values 0-255). Real code pattern: def get_byte_count(n, table): return table[n & 0xFF]. Connection to L2: This relies on the core logic of byte-wise processing.

examples.trace: Input: n = 42, table precomputed. Step 1: Extract byte: 42 & 0xFF = 42 (since 42 in binary is 00101010, mask 11111111 keeps all bits). Step 2: Lookup: table[42] = 3. Return 3.

Input: n = 300 (binary 100101100). Step 1: Extract lowest byte: 300 & 0xFF = 44 (since 300 in binary: ...00101100, last 8 bits are 00101100 = 44). Step 2: Lookup: table[44] = 3 (assuming precomputed). Return 3.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Byte-wise Processing with Right Shift
ladders.desc: Technique of breaking integer into bytes by repeatedly extracting lowest byte and shifting
ladders.explanation: This core logic enables handling integers of any size by processing them one byte at a time. The intuition is that an integer's binary representation can be divided into fixed-size chunks (bytes), and we can process each chunk independently. By repeatedly taking the lowest byte and then right-shifting the integer, we move to the next byte until the integer becomes zero. This is efficient because shifting and masking are fast bitwise operations. The algorithm: 1. Input: integer n. 2. While n != 0: a. Process current byte (e.g., use lookup table). b. Right shift n by 8 bits: n = n >> 8. 3. End when n == 0. Time complexity is O(b) where b is number of bytes (e.g., 4 for 32-bit). Space is O(1). This connects to L1 by providing the loop structure that calls the byte extraction. Common mistakes include infinite loop if using signed shift with negative numbers (use unsigned shift or mask), off-by-one errors in shift amount, and not handling zero correctly. Real code pattern: while n: byte = n & 0xFF; process(byte); n >>= 8. Connection to L3: This relies on bitwise operations as building blocks.

examples.trace: Input: n = 42 (binary 00101010). Step 1: n != 0, extract byte: 42 & 0xFF = 42. Step 2: Process byte 42. Step 3: Right shift: n >>= 8 gives 0. Step 4: n == 0, loop ends. Bytes processed: [42].

Input: n = 300 (binary 00000001 00101100). Step 1: n != 0, extract byte: 300 & 0xFF = 44 (lowest 8 bits). Step 2: Process byte 44. Step 3: Right shift: n >>= 8 gives 1. Step 4: n != 0, extract byte: 1 & 0xFF = 1. Step 5: Process byte 1. Step 6: Right shift: n >>= 8 gives 0. Step 7: n == 0, loop ends. Bytes processed: [44, 1].

ladders.title: Building Block — Bitwise AND and Right Shift Operations
ladders.desc: Using bitwise operators to manipulate binary data
ladders.explanation: This building block involves fundamental bitwise operations essential for byte-wise processing. The intuition is that computers represent integers in binary, and bitwise operators allow direct manipulation of these bits for efficiency. AND (&) is used to mask bits: n & mask keeps only bits where mask has 1s. For byte extraction, mask = 0xFF (binary 11111111) keeps the lowest 8 bits. Right shift (>>) moves bits to the right, discarding the lowest bits and filling with zeros (for unsigned) or sign bit (for signed), effectively dividing by 2^k for k shifts. These operations are fast because they map directly to processor instructions. The algorithm: 1. Masking: result = n & 0xFF to get lowest byte. 2. Shifting: n = n >> 8 to remove processed byte. Time complexity O(1) per operation. Space O(1). This connects to L2 by enabling the byte extraction and shift in the loop. Common mistakes include using signed shift with negative numbers (can cause infinite loops), incorrect mask values, and misunderstanding operator precedence. Real code pattern: byte = n & 0xFF; n = n >> 8. Connection to L4: These operations work on binary representations.

examples.trace: Input: n = 300 (binary 00000001 00101100), mask = 0xFF (11111111). Step 1: AND: lowest 8 bits are 00101100 = 44. Step 2: Right shift by 8: removes lowest byte, result is 00000001 = 1.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Binary Representation and Bit Manipulation
ladders.desc: Understanding how integers are stored in binary and accessing individual bits
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves the foundation of how computers represent integers in binary (base-2) and how to access specific bits. The intuition is that every integer can be expressed as a sequence of bits (0s and 1s), where each bit represents a power of 2. For example, the integer 5 is binary 101, meaning 1*2^2 + 0*2^1 + 1*2^0. Bit manipulation refers to operations that work on these bits directly, such as checking if a bit is set (1) or clear (0). To access a bit at position i (0-indexed from right), we can use (n >> i) & 1: right shift moves the bit to the least significant position, AND with 1 isolates it. This is crucial for counting set bits. The algorithm: 1. For integer n and position i, compute bit = (n >> i) & 1. 2. bit will be 0 or 1. Time O(1) per bit, space O(1). This connects to L3 by providing the basis for masking and shifting. Common mistakes include off-by-one in bit positions, misunderstanding bit order (LSB vs MSB), and not using parentheses due to operator precedence. Real code pattern: bit = (n >> i) & 1. Connection to L5: This builds on the concept of binary numbers.

examples.trace: Input: n = 5 (binary 101), i = 0 (least significant bit). Step 1: Right shift by 0: 101 >> 0 = 101. Step 2: AND with 1: 101 & 001 = 001 = 1. So bit at position 0 is 1.
Input: n = 5 (binary 101), i = 1. Step 1: Right shift by 1: 101 >> 1 = 010 (binary 10). Step 2: AND with 1: 010 & 001 = 000 = 0. So bit at position 1 is 0.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Binary Number System and Bit Values
ladders.desc: Introduction to binary representation and what bits are
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: understanding the binary number system. The intuition is that computers use binary (base-2) to represent data, where each digit is a bit (binary digit) that can be 0 or 1. A binary number is a sequence of bits, with each position representing a power of 2, starting from 2^0 on the right. For example, the binary number 1011 means 1*2^3 + 0*2^2 + 1*2^1 + 1*2^0 = 8 + 0 + 2 + 1 = 11 in decimal. A set bit is a bit with value 1. Counting set bits means counting how many 1s are in the binary representation. This foundation is essential because the problem involves manipulating these bits. The algorithm: To convert decimal to binary, repeatedly divide by 2 and record remainders. To count set bits naively, convert to binary and count 1s. Time O(log n) for conversion, space O(log n) for storage. This connects to L4 by providing the basis for bit manipulation. Common mistakes include confusing binary with decimal, incorrect bit positions, and not understanding that integers have fixed bit sizes in computers. Real code pattern: Learn to represent numbers in binary and identify bits. Connection to higher levels: This is the starting point for all bitwise operations.

examples.trace: Input: decimal number 5. Step 1: Convert to binary: 5 / 2 = 2 remainder 1, 2 / 2 = 1 remainder 0, 1 / 2 = 0 remainder 1. Read remainders backwards: 101. Step 2: Count 1s: bits are 1, 0, 1, so two 1s. Set bits = 2.

Input: decimal number 10. Step 1: Convert to binary: 10 / 2 = 5 remainder 0, 5 / 2 = 2 remainder 1, 2 / 2 = 1 remainder 0, 1 / 2 = 0 remainder 1. Remainders: 0,1,0,1 backwards: 1010. Step 2: Count 1s: bits are 1,0,1,0, so two 1s. Set bits = 2.

approaches.name: Built-in Population Count O(1) hardware
approaches.summary: Use hardware-optimized instructions like __builtin_popcount in C/C++ or Integer.bitCount in Java, which are implemented at the CPU level for maximum efficiency. This is the fastest method but relies on language-specific features.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(1) typically constant-time due to hardware support

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count Set Bits Using Hardware-Optimized Built-in Functions
ladders.desc: Complete solution using language-specific hardware-optimized population count functions.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete solution to counting set bits (1s) in binary representation using hardware-optimized built-in functions. The intuition is that modern CPUs have dedicated instructions (like POPCNT on x86) that can count set bits in a single operation, making this the fastest possible approach. These instructions are exposed through language-specific built-in functions that compile directly to the CPU instruction. The algorithm is straightforward: take an integer input, call the built-in population count function, and return the result. Time complexity is O(1) since it's a single hardware instruction, and space complexity is O(1) as no additional data structures are needed. This connects directly to the key subroutine (L1) which is the built-in function call itself. Common mistakes include using these functions without understanding their platform dependence (they may not be available on all architectures) or assuming they work with negative numbers (some implementations handle two's complement, others don't). Always check your language's documentation for edge cases. Real code pattern: In Python-style pseudocode, we'd use a hypothetical built-in function since Python doesn't have one natively, but in languages like C/C++ you'd use __builtin_popcount(n).

examples.trace: Input: 13 (binary 1101). Step 1: Call built-in population count function on integer 13. Step 2: Function internally uses CPU instruction to count bits: 1101 has bits at positions 0, 2, and 3 set → 3 set bits. Step 3: Return 3.
Input: 255 (binary 11111111). Step 1: Call built-in population count function on integer 255. Step 2: Function counts all 8 bits as set. Step 3: Return 8.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Calling Hardware Population Count Function
ladders.desc: The built-in function call that leverages CPU instructions for bit counting.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the key subroutine: calling the hardware-optimized built-in population count function. The intuition is that instead of implementing bit-counting logic in software, we delegate to the CPU's specialized instruction, which is extremely fast. The algorithm involves invoking a language-specific function (e.g., __builtin_popcount in C/C++, Integer.bitCount in Java, or similar). Step-by-step: 1. Ensure the input is an integer. 2. Call the function with the integer as argument. 3. The function executes a single CPU instruction (like POPCNT) that counts set bits. 4. Return the result. Time complexity is O(1) as it's a constant-time hardware operation, and space is O(1). This feeds into L0 as the core operation of the full solution. Common mistakes include not including necessary headers (in C/C++) or using the wrong function name for the language. Also, some functions may have undefined behavior for negative numbers—always verify in documentation. Real code pattern: In Python-style pseudocode, imagine a function popcount(n) that maps to hardware, e.g., result = hardware_popcount(n).

examples.trace: Input: 5 (binary 101). Step 1: Call hardware_popcount(5). Step 2: CPU instruction counts bits: positions 0 and 2 are set → 2. Step 3: Return 2.
Input: 8 (binary 1000). Step 1: Call hardware_popcount(8). Step 2: CPU counts only bit at position 3 set → 1. Step 3: Return 1.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Understanding Hardware Population Count Instructions
ladders.desc: The central insight of using CPU-level instructions for efficient bit counting.
ladders.explanation: This level explains the core logic: using hardware population count instructions. The intuition is that counting set bits is a common operation in computing (e.g., for cryptography, error correction), so CPUs have dedicated instructions to do it in one cycle, avoiding slower software loops. The algorithm at this conceptual level is: the CPU takes an integer register value, applies the POPCNT instruction (or equivalent), and returns the count in another register. Step-by-step in pseudocode: 1. Load integer into a CPU register. 2. Execute POPCNT instruction. 3. Store result in output register. Time is O(1) due to single instruction, space O(1). This connects to L1 as the underlying mechanism of the built-in function. Common mistakes include assuming all CPUs support this (it's available on modern x86 with SSE4.2, but not on older or some embedded systems) or misunderstanding the instruction's behavior with different data sizes (e.g., 32-bit vs 64-bit). Real code pattern: In assembly-like pseudocode, e.g., POPCNT result, input.

examples.trace: Input: 11 (binary 1011). Step 1: Load 11 into register R1. Step 2: Execute POPCNT on R1, counting bits: positions 0, 1, and 3 are set → 3. Step 3: Store 3 in result register R2.
Input: 24 (binary 11000). Step 1: Load 24 into R1. Step 2: POPCNT counts bits at positions 3 and 4 set → 2. Step 3: Store 2 in R2.

ladders.title: Building Block — Binary Representation of Integers
ladders.desc: Understanding how integers are represented in binary form.
ladders.explanation: This level covers the building block: binary representation of integers. The intuition is that to count set bits, we must first understand how numbers are stored in binary, where each bit represents a power of two. The algorithm involves converting a decimal integer to its binary equivalent. Step-by-step: 1. Take a non-negative integer n. 2. Repeatedly divide n by 2, recording remainders (0 or 1) until n becomes 0. 3. The remainders in reverse order give the binary representation. Time complexity is O(log n) for the conversion, space O(log n) to store bits. This feeds into L2 as hardware instructions operate on this binary form. Common mistakes include confusion with signed integers (two's complement) or off-by-one errors in bit positions. Real code pattern: In Python-style pseudocode, e.g., bits = [] while n > 0: bits.append(n % 2); n = n // 2; reverse bits.

examples.trace: Input: 6. Step 1: 6 ÷ 2 = 3 remainder 0. Step 2: 3 ÷ 2 = 1 remainder 1. Step 3: 1 ÷ 2 = 0 remainder 1. Remainders: 0, 1, 1. Reverse: 110 (binary).
Input: 13. Step 1: 13 ÷ 2 = 6 remainder 1. Step 2: 6 ÷ 2 = 3 remainder 0. Step 3: 3 ÷ 2 = 1 remainder 1. Step 4: 1 ÷ 2 = 0 remainder 1. Remainders: 1, 0, 1, 1. Reverse: 1101.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Bitwise AND with 1 to Check LSB
ladders.desc: Using bitwise AND to determine if the least significant bit is set.
ladders.explanation: This level introduces a basic operation: using bitwise AND with 1 to check if the least significant bit (LSB) is set. The intuition is that in binary, the LSB (rightmost bit) determines if a number is odd (1) or even (0), and we can isolate it using AND with 1. The algorithm: for an integer n, compute n & 1; if result is 1, LSB is set; if 0, it's not. Step-by-step: 1. Take integer n. 2. Perform bitwise AND: n & 1. 3. Interpret result: 1 means LSB is 1, 0 means LSB is 0. Time O(1), space O(1). This connects to L3 as a step in analyzing binary digits. Common mistakes include confusing & (bitwise AND) with && (logical AND) or not understanding that this only checks the LSB. Real code pattern: In Python-style pseudocode, e.g., lsb_set = (n & 1) == 1.

examples.trace: Input: 5 (binary 101). Step 1: Compute 5 & 1. In binary: 101 AND 001 = 001. Step 2: Result is 1, so LSB is set.
Input: 4 (binary 100). Step 1: Compute 4 & 1. In binary: 100 AND 001 = 000. Step 2: Result is 0, so LSB is not set.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Binary Digits (Bits) and Place Value
ladders.desc: Understanding bits as binary digits and their place values in base-2.
ladders.explanation: This is the foundation concept: binary digits (bits) and their place values. The intuition is that computers use binary (base-2) where each digit (bit) is either 0 or 1, and positions represent powers of two, similar to decimal digits representing powers of ten. The algorithm involves expressing a number in binary: for a number n, the bit at position i (from right, starting at 0) contributes 2^i if set to 1. Step-by-step: 1. Identify place values: 2^0=1, 2^1=2, 2^2=4, etc. 2. For a given number, determine which bits are 1 by seeing how it can be summed from these powers. Time and space are O(1) for basic understanding. This feeds into L4 as the basis for bitwise operations. Common mistakes include mixing up binary with decimal or misunderstanding that leading zeros don't change the value. Real code pattern: In conceptual pseudocode, e.g., binary of 5 is 101 because 4 (2^2) + 0 (2^1) + 1 (2^0) = 5.

examples.trace: Input: 3. Step 1: Place values: 2^1=2, 2^0=1. Step 2: 3 = 2 + 1, so bits: at position 1: 1, at position 0: 1. Binary: 11.
Input: 10. Step 1: Place values: 2^3=8, 2^2=4, 2^1=2, 2^0=1. Step 2: 10 = 8 + 2, so bits: position 3: 1, position 2: 0, position 1: 1, position 0: 0. Binary: 1010.
